rounds.blurb: Space Shuttle Challenger breaks apart 73 seconds after launch.
rounds.credit: NASA / Kennedy Space Center — public domain
rounds.story: A booster O-ring, stiffened by an overnight freeze, failed 73 seconds after liftoff. The loss of all seven crew — including teacher Christa McAuliffe, with schoolchildren watching live — grounded the fleet for almost three years.

rounds.blurb: A U.S. Navy F-14 over Kuwait's burning oil wells in the months after the Gulf War.
rounds.credit: Lt. Steve Gozzo, U.S. Navy — public domain
rounds.story: Retreating forces set more than 600 Kuwaiti wells alight; the smoke turned midday to dusk and the fires burned most of a year. Crews from ten nations capped them faster than any forecast predicted.

rounds.blurb: The crowd on the National Mall for Barack Obama's first inauguration.
rounds.story: An estimated 1.8 million people packed the Mall in sub-freezing cold — the largest crowd ever recorded in Washington. The Metro carried more riders that day than on any day before it.

rounds.blurb: Charles Lindbergh with the Spirit of St. Louis, the year of the first solo transatlantic flight.
rounds.story: Days before this portrait circulated, the 25-year-old airmail pilot flew the Spirit of St. Louis nonstop from New York to Paris: 33 and a half hours, alone, navigating by dead reckoning. He took off an unknown and landed to a crowd of 150,000.

rounds.blurb: Riders in a spray-painted New York subway car, photographed for the EPA's Documerica project.
rounds.credit: Erik Calonius / EPA Documerica, National Archives — public domain
rounds.story: Erik Calonius photographed New York's spray-painted fleet for the EPA's Documerica project, just as graffiti writing exploded from teenage fad into art movement. The MTA spent the next two decades trying to scrub it out.
